stem: The nurse is caring for a 15-year-old client who is 6 hours post-reduction of a tibial fracture and is now reporting severe, uncontrolled pain. Based on the provided electronic health record, the client is developing a serious complication. Complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: This is the nurse for the 15-year-old male in room 204, who is 6 hours post-tibial fracture reduction. He is reporting severe, uncontrolled pain rated 10/10.
Background: He sustained a closed tibial fracture during a soccer game. A closed reduction and plaster cast were applied in the ED. He received IV morphine at 13:30 with no relief.
Assessment: At 14:00, neurovascular check reveals pain on passive toe extension, paresthesia in the foot, and tense, shiny skin over the lower leg. Distal pulses are 2+. Vitals: HR 112, BP 124/80, RR 20, SpO2 99%.
Recommendation: I suspect acute compartment syndrome. I request an immediate orthopedic consult for evaluation and possible fasciotomy.

tabs.title: Neurovascular Flowsheet
tabs.content:
Assessment ItemCurrent FindingInterpretation
Pain Level10/10 (Persistent)Pain out of proportion
Passive MotionPain with passive extension of toesClassic early sign
Pulse2+ (Present)LATE sign (Can still be positive)
Paresthesia'Pins and needles' in toesNerve compression
SkinPale; Tense; ShinyHigh pressure

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Initial X-ray confirmed tibial fracture. Post-reduction films not available.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Morphine 4mg IV every 2 hours PRN pain. Last dose given at 13:30.

actionOptions.text: Loosen or cut the restrictive dressing or cast
Maintain the affected limb at the level of the heart
Elevate the limb above the level of the heart
Apply ice packs and maintain high-pressure compression

hoverRationales.text: DVT typically presents with unilateral swelling, warmth, and redness, not the severe pain on passive motion and paresthesia seen here. The client's symptoms are more indicative of neurovascular compromise from pressure.

Increased pressure within a fascial compartment compromises circulation and nerve function. This leads to pain out of proportion to the injury, pain with passive stretch, and paresthesia, which are classic signs of this condition.
Pain that is unrelieved by opioids and rated 10/10, along with new-onset paresthesia, is not a normal finding. These are red flags for a serious complication requiring immediate intervention.
Removing the external source of compression is the first step to decrease intracompartmental pressure. This can restore blood flow and prevent irreversible muscle and nerve damage.
Keeping the limb at heart level (neutral position) optimizes arterial inflow without increasing venous pressure and edema. This position helps maintain perfusion to the compromised tissues.
Elevating the limb above the heart will decrease arterial perfusion to an already ischemic area. This action would worsen the effects of compartment syndrome.
Applying more compression would further increase the already dangerously high intracompartmental pressure. While ice can reduce swelling, adding compression is contraindicated.

Frequent assessment of the 6 Ps (pain, paresthesia, pallor, paralysis, pulselessness, poikilothermia) is critical to monitor for changes in perfusion and nerve function. Worsening signs indicate progression of the syndrome.

Direct measurement of pressure within the muscle compartment provides a definitive diagnosis. Pressures greater than 30 mmHg are typically indicative of compartment syndrome and the need for fasciotomy.

While leg circumference may increase, it is not a precise or timely indicator of rising intracompartmental pressure. Neurovascular checks and direct pressure monitoring are far more sensitive and specific.
Temperature and WBC count are markers for infection, such as osteomyelitis, not acute compartment syndrome. This is a perfusion emergency, not an infectious one.
